figures.caption: Table 2 | Ablation study. Isolation R&E (✓ = each stage has its own router and experts, × = a single shared group across all stages) and MoE (✓ = multiple experts, × = a single expert).

Figure 4 | t-SNE visualization of latent memories produced by different experts on Math prompts. Different experts occupy distinct regions of the t-SNE space, indicating diverse learned memory representations.
Figure 5 | t-SNE visualization of latent memories produced by the corresponding expert (within-pool index 0) on math, science, and code prompts.

Table 4 | Continual learning results under Order A: Code → Science → Math. All methods share Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 as the base model. After each training stage we report per-test accuracy and three CL auxiliary metrics. Auxiliary metrics are undefined at 𝑡 = 1 and shown as “–”. Bold marks the best score within each stage. Subscripts on Average mark the gap relative to the Vanilla baseline (+/−).

Table 5 | Continual learning results under Order B: Science → Code → Math. All methods share Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 as the base model. After each training stage we report per-test accuracy and three CL auxiliary metrics. Auxiliary metrics are undefined at 𝑡 = 1 and shown as “–”. Bold marks the best score within each stage. Subscripts on Average mark the gap relative to the Vanilla baseline (+/−).

Table 3 | AE routing statistics across continual-learning stages. At each stage, the prompt-end feature is compared only against the autoencoders trained on already-introduced domains; samples far from every available domain AE are rejected as OOD and fall back to the pretrained model.
Figure 3 | Overall expert usage on the Math, Science, and Code test sets.

Table 1 | Continual learning results under the default order Math → Science → Code. All methods share Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 as the base model. After each training stage we report per-test accuracy and three CL auxiliary metrics. Auxiliary metrics are undefined at 𝑡 = 1 and shown as “–”. Bold marks the best score within each stage. Subscripts on Average mark the gap relative to the Vanilla baseline (+/−).

Figure 1 | Overview of MoLEM. Each expert is attached to the frozen base reasoner and produces a candidate latent memory. The router performs key-query matching to compute expert weights and aggregates the candidates into the final latent memory that is injected back into the reasoner. For continual learning, each stage is paired with a lightweight autoencoder (AE); at inference, the AE with the smallest reconstruction error selects the corresponding routing group, while inputs whose reconstruction errors all exceed their thresholds are classified as out-of-distribution and fall back to the pretrained model.
Figure 2 | t-SNE visualization of frozen-reasoner hidden states at the first latent-memory insertion point for math, science, and code training prompts.
